_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 1
choices: It does not compile
It throws a NullPointerException
questions.explanation: `new` was called once, so there is exactly one object. `Student b = a;` copies the reference, not the object, so `a` and `b` are two names pointing at the same Student. Changing it through `b` changes what you see through `a`, and the answer is 2.0. If you picked 3.9, you read `b = a` as making a second student. It does not: only `new` builds an object, and there is no second `new` here.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 2
questions.prompt: The Student class declares `String name;` and `double gpa;` and has no constructor. What does this print?
choices: null then 0.0
It throws a NullPointerException on the first println
It does not compile, since the fields were never set
An empty line then 0
questions.explanation: `new` really did build an object, so `s` points at something and the dot works. Java fills unset fields with defaults: a `double` starts at 0.0, and any object type, including `String`, starts at `null`. So you get `null` and then `0.0`. If you picked the NullPointerException, you had the right idea about `null` but the wrong target: `s.name` is null, and `s` is not. Printing a null is fine. Only using a dot on a null crashes.

questions.title: Trace Classes & Objects
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The whole file. `name` and `gpa` are fields: the state each Student carries, declared once in the class and then owned separately by every object built from it. `new Student()` builds one such object and hands back a reference to it, which is what `s` holds. The resulting output is 'Ada\n3.9'.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 5
choices: It prints "here", then throws a NullPointerException
It prints "here", then an empty line
It prints nothing and throws immediately
It fails to compile
questions.explanation: `s` is declared but points at no object, so the first line runs and prints "here". The second line asks the object at `s` for its `name`, but there is no object, so Java throws a NullPointerException at that line. It compiles cleanly, which is what makes the bug survive to run time.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 1
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "You have been writing classes all along"?
choices: Every Java program you have written so far sat inside `public class Something { ... }`.
It does not compile
It throws a NullPointerException
questions.explanation: A class is a blueprint: it lists the fields an object will hold and the methods it can run. An object is one actual thing built from that blueprint. That reasoning supports the answer: Every Java program you have written so far sat inside `public class Something { ... }`.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 2
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "What `this` is for"?
choices: It throws a NullPointerException on the first println
It does not compile, since the fields were never set
In that constructor, the parameter is called `name` and the field is also called `name`. Java resolves the plain name `name` to the parameter, because the closer one wins.
An empty line then 0
questions.explanation: A class is a blueprint: it lists the fields an object will hold and the methods it can run. An object is one actual thing built from that blueprint. That reasoning supports the answer: In that constructor, the parameter is called `name` and the field is also called `name`. Java resolves the plain name `name` to the parameter, because the closer one wins.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 3
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "A NullPointerException means you never called new"?
choices: It throws a NullPointerException
It throws a NullPointerException on the first println
This is the error Java beginners hit most. It does not mean the object is broken.
questions.explanation: A class is a blueprint: it lists the fields an object will hold and the methods it can run. An object is one actual thing built from that blueprint. That reasoning supports the answer: This is the error Java beginners hit most. It does not mean the object is broken.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 4
questions.prompt: Which guideline helps prevent a common Classes & Objects mistake?
choices: Name the class for the thing it represents, capitalized: `Student`, not `doStudentStuff`.
It does not compile, since the fields were never set
An empty line then 0
questions.explanation: A class is a blueprint: it lists the fields an object will hold and the methods it can run. An object is one actual thing built from that blueprint. That reasoning supports the answer: Name the class for the thing it represents, capitalized: `Student`, not `doStudentStuff`.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 5
questions.prompt: A classmate is checking their understanding of Classes & Objects. Which advice is correct?
choices: Fields are what it knows. Methods are what it can do. Sort your ideas into those two piles first.
It does not compile
It does not compile, since the fields were never set
questions.explanation: A class is a blueprint: it lists the fields an object will hold and the methods it can run. An object is one actual thing built from that blueprint. That reasoning supports the answer: Fields are what it knows. Methods are what it can do. Sort your ideas into those two piles first.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 6
questions.prompt: Which choice shows sound reasoning about Classes & Objects?
choices: An empty line then 0
Write a constructor as soon as an object needs more than one field, so an object can never exist half filled in.
questions.explanation: A class is a blueprint: it lists the fields an object will hold and the methods it can run. An object is one actual thing built from that blueprint. That reasoning supports the answer: Write a constructor as soon as an object needs more than one field, so an object can never exist half filled in.
